data.title: NuScale Power: Death Spiral Risk (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
NuScale Power (SMR) is the stock I regret the most. I still had hopes that I was wrong when I covered it last year, but I should have shorted. At the time, my position was pragmatic; I decided to have some confidence in the management and wait till the year 2025 ended. To be fair, the hope was built on sharp movement up in case they signed 2-3 clients as the market was waiting for evidence, and so did I.

But today, I would like to revisit my last thesis and do some reality checks. The year has ended, and the main catalyst—contracts—did not appear. The market has punished the company since then, almost 50% down. Now I believe this ~$16 per share price is becoming dangerous. I have seen in other forums that investors were left as bagholders in the company that is actively burning cash, and the horizon for catalysts seems to be empty. I believe it is not a risky growth story anymore, but given current market conditions, I hate to call anything like that, but it is dead money.

What I Was Expecting

Let's revisit what I was expecting last time. Last time I was writing that the management was expecting firm orders till the end of 2025. This was a KPI that I was hoping they would reach. Without real commercial contracts, NuScale technology, even though innovative, in financial terms remains just an expensive science project.

I believe that the market was pricing in this hope too. I remember that one of the readers summarized bull sentiment perfectly: “The share price will be above $60 if they manage to get at least one real contract by the year's end.” At the time, $36 per share was already pricing in the perfect scenario. To be fair, it might have been the reason why I was rating it a hold rather than a sell.

Sadly, 2025 ended without any breaking news, which I was hoping for. It's important to understand when trading story stocks that in capital markets, when a company publicly promises to reach a goal and gives the date, silence is not neutral, and it ends up in a sell-off, speaking from my experience. The silence when the public is awaiting results is considered bad news. This pause that we are seeing is signaling that either conditions are not satisfying the clients or the clients are cautious about new technology for commercial use. As I said, the market hates uncertainty, and at this time, the silence was louder than any words.

Dilution Maths

This might be the most important argument why I am changing this stock’s rating to strong sell. The technology is still the same, projects are still the same, but the financial structure, I believe, is becoming a trap. In August 2025, when I wrote my second thesis, the hold rating was based on the company's solid cash reserve, which should have lasted them for a long time. Let's not forget the quite high share price (~$36), which also allowed me to expect that any capital attraction would be minimally painful to investors. Sadly, the math changed.

Look at the numbers. Let's say that for NuScale to continue its operations and project development, they need $300 million (this is quite realistic for this type of company).

  • Situation in August 2025 (share price $36), to get $300 million, the company needed around 8.3 million new shares. That would have been a painful but understandable 6-7% shareholder dilution.
  • Now, at the current price ($16), to attract the same $300 million, the company would be forced to issue almost 18 million new shares.

Given that they like this type of financing, it means that the capital became twice as expensive. Every dollar that NuScale is spending is costing twice the price it was 6 months ago. In my opinion, it creates a classical death spiral. This happens when the share prices are dropping and the company is forced to issue more shares to reach the same result.

Q3 Did Not Give Hope Either

If in August I was speculating about potential, in November the company announced Q3 results, which were, let's call them, bad. The market was expecting that the loss per share (EPS) would be around -$0.13; sadly, it was -$1.85. So why am I saying it's bad? Because it was a negative surprise of 1300%. The revenue is stagnating. Even though it did rise to $8.2 million, it still does not meet analyst expectations ($11.5 million). It's important to understand that all the revenue comes from engineering services for current projects and not from sales. This proved my main fear that NuScale is more consultancy than industrial nuclear reactor manufacturer.

Strategic Partnership

One of the reasons I believe it is a strong sell is that the main shareholder, Fluor (FLR), decided to sell part of their shares. They sold at the peak, earning around $600 million, and officially announced their plans to sell the remaining shares until mid-2026. So, we are going to see pressure on the stock price either way; if it's going to go up due to contracts, FLR will just be selling at higher prices, which would become pressure. Traders call it resistance.

When the partner, which knows technology and commercial preparedness, is trying to get out, this becomes a red flag. This is a signal, that they do not believe in this investment or they know something we don't.

Catalysts

Currently, I believe the only hope is the RoPower project in Romania, but in a shareholder meeting that should be held on February 12-13 about the final FID. But I believe it is just another bureaucratic step. I believe that even in the best scenario, the first reactor will start operating only in 2027-2028, but this is my opinion. Given this, I also expect 2 years of constant cash burn without any profitability.

At this time, NuScale is using the ATM program; in Q3, they issued 13.2 million new shares to attract the capital. In December, they issued approval to increase authorized shares from 332 million to 662 million, and I think that is final proof that dilution is not only being continued, but it is going to continue in the future. While I did not talk about many catalysts here, it's simply because I can't; there are no potential catalysts that I see.

Summary

My hold rating in August was a mistake. I tried to be more conservative and trusted that the management would be able to convert the experiment to commercial contracts. Today, I see only bad results: EPS is going down, the main partner is leaving, and dilution is almost guaranteed to happen again. At the current price, I still believe there is room to go down, and I will be changing my rating to strong sell. I believe that NuScale became an example of how an innovative company can become a financial void for investors.

data.title: 2 BDCs To Dump Before They Cut Their Dividend
data.description:
The business development company, or BDC, market (BIZD) has already for some time fallen out of investors' favor, at least from the marginal buyer perspective.

The first repricing started to happen in early 2025, when the market realized that lower base rates will eventually erode BDC earrings power, which in turn would lead to dividend reductions. Another element that supported BDC deratings was the notable spread compression as the M&A markets were effectively idle due to interest rate and policy uncertainty.

Then in Q3, 2025, Jamie Dimon came out with the famous comment around additional cockroaches emerging in the seemingly risk-loaded private credit space. This was triggered by the Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies, which in combination with Jamie's comment caused notable shockwaves in the sector.

However, after some period of structural repricing, in late 2025 BDCs finally started to exhibit signs of a reversal, which, in my view, was fully warranted as the BDC portfolio qualities still remained robust and the dividend yields (even after some cuts considered) were extremely competitive in relation to other high-yield alternatives.

Now, because of the increased chatter around SaaS companies (IGV) and how they could get impaired due to the AI technologies, the market has once again switched on the "BDC punishment" mode.

All of this just shows that BDC prices are mostly (at least currently) dictated by the credit risk dynamics and not what takes place at the interest rate (and thus dividend) department.

Having said that, it does not mean that we should ignore the dividend component. For many BDC investors the expectation that gets assigned to the deployed capital here is twofold:

  1. Accessing high dividends that are not fed by the underlying NAV.
  2. Getting that income in steady and reliable fashion.

And in this context, I have to say that the overall picture has become quite complex. It is not that black of white.

While there are several BDCs - e.g., Ares Capital (ARCC) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) - that have just reported better than expected earnings and remained committed on the current dividends, the headwinds have not disappeared and the sector average base dividend coverage and debt levels do not send comforting signals at all.

For example, Gladstone Capital (GLAD), Golub Capital (GBDC) (I had a warning article on this out) and Palmer Square Capital BDC (PSBD) have already cut their dividends. And most BDCs have not yet reported their Q4 figures.

This brings me to the following two BDCs, which, in my humble opinion, are very likely to surprise dividend income investors to the downside.

#1: OBDC, 13.1%

Back in July, 2024, I wrote a contrarian piece on Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), suggesting that the discount was too small in the context of risks and very thin margin of safety. Since then OBDC has dropped by more than 20%, which is way worse than what the overall sector has experienced.

Currently, we can buy OBDC at ~27% discount to NAV, which is one of the deepest discount levels that OBDC has ever had apart from the COVID-19 moment. This discount is even more aggressive than what has been baked into the cake for sector average - i.e., P/NAV of 0.81x.

We could debate whether this kind of pricing is too aggressive or not, but one thing is clear, the presence of a significant discount is definitely justified, where part of the rationale could be linked to unsustainable dividend.

Let me explain why I think that OBDC is on the brink of slashing its dividend.

First, the adjusted NII per share is already below the quarterly base dividend. Based on Q3, 2025 data, the dividend coverage is ~97%, which means that OBDC has to tap into its balance sheet to close the cash flow gap.

Second, of the total income generation, about 6.4% comes in the form of PIK, making the actual cash flow gap even worse.

Third, speaking of leverage and OBDC's ability to use debt to keep the dividend running, the picture does not look great. As of Q3, 2025, OBDC had a debt to equity of 1.22x, which is very close the usual debt ceiling of 1.25x. It is clear that OBDC has not the necessary capacity to support the current dividend unless the NII per share goes up, which leads me to the final point.

Fourth, the prospects for NII per share result going up are quite weak. In Q4, 2025 OBDC will be reporting financial that will already include the effects from the previous three base rate cuts that were not reflected in the Q3 data. These reductions are inherently negative to the NII generation, which we can nicely see in the NII trajectory from Q4, 2025 to Q3, 2025 (i.e., a material drop, mostly driven by less supportive SOFR).

The only way OBDC could offset these forthcoming pressures is through portfolio expansion, which in turn can happen via additional debt or additional equity funding. The former, as discussed, is not an option. The latter is not an option either given how deep the discount is (i.e., raising fresh equity below NAV would be destructive for the existing shareholder base).

This is very similar story to what I was telling about GBDC before the BDC announced an interest rate driven dividend cut.

If I was looking for high and reliable/stable yield, I would not go with OBDC.

#2: OCSL, 12.9%

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) is another BDC, which I think is about to cut its dividend. Yet, as opposed to OBDC, in this case OCSL has actually relatively recently made adjustments to its base dividend.

It happened just as I predicted in February, 2025 that very likely because of rising non-accruals and unfavorable debt refinancings OCSL will be forced to recalibrate the quarterly distribution amount in order to avoid using additional debt for dividend payment purposes.

However, looking at the current financials, I have to say that one cut will not probably be enough to fully stabilize the situation. In fact, the discount here is almost the same as for OBDC (P/NAV of 0.73x for OCSL), which is an indicative of elevated market fears.

Now, if we look at the current base dividend coverage level, we will arrive at a slightly better conclusion than for OBDC. Namely, the coverage level stands at 103%, which does not signal an immediate need for the balance sheet side to step in.

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, the actual status is arguably even worse than for OBDC.

Here is the thing.

First, just as for OBDC, this BDC has also around 6.5% of its income stemming from PIK, which means that cash dividend coverage is below 100%.

Second, while the recently reported NII per share figure came in at $0.01 per share above the result that was achieved in the previous quarter, in reality, the top-line has decreased. The only driver for the positive NII per share development was a notable adjustment to the Part 1 incentive fee, which is only a temporary solution. Excluding this component, the underlying NII per share would have contracted by ~$0.01 per share, throwing the base dividend coverage level below 100%.

Third, we have to appreciate that the recent financial report includes only partial effects from the realized base rate cuts. Usually, there is at least a couple of months of lag between the moment when the Fed cuts and when the consequences percolate through BDC books. I doubt that in the next quarter we will see any NII per share growth.

Fourth, and this is the most important point, OCSL continues to struggle with its loan book. For example, in the recent figures we can see that the BDC has yet again registered declining NAV (this time ~$0.34 per share) due to several markdowns and complete write-offs. Given that around 23% of OCSL's total loan book is tied to SaaS businesses, which have on a weighted average basis only 1.8x of interest coverage (high quality BDCs have 2.0x and above), I would not rule out incremental non-accruals. In such case, the dividend cut would become an instant topic instead of a question of most likely couple of quarters.

In a nutshell, in my view, OCSL is a great example that shows how one dividend cut does not automatically guarantee that the next years will be safe and result in truly durable current income streams.

data.title: WGMI: High Correlation To Bitcoin, Significant Downside Remaining
data.description:
Thesis

The CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (WGMI) is an equities exchange traded fund. In particular, the ETF aims to:

Gain pure-play access to North America's leading Bitcoin mining industry, a crucial component of the Bitcoin ecosystem. WGMI provides targeted exposure to the companies at the forefront of transaction verification, ensuring transparency and security on the blockchain.

The ETF is dedicated to companies in the bitcoin mining industry, and comes with a high beta to the S&P 500. Given the recent volatility in bitcoin and down momentum for the crypto universe, we are going to have a closer look at this fund and its outlook in today's macro.

Fund composition

Let us start by looking at the holdings in this ETF:

The above table has been extracted by the author from the list present on the ETF's website. Note that the top ten holdings make up over 82% of the ETF, with a large concentration in Cipher Mining (CIFR) and IREN Ltd (IREN). The fund is actively managed, with a reported 40% portfolio turnover, however do note the universe of crypto miners is fairly limited.

Before we take a close look at the balance sheets and income statements of some of the largest exposures here, let us establish the correlation to bitcoin for this ETF.

Correlation to Bitcoin

Let us look a little closer at how the ETF does versus an outright play on bitcoin via the largest ETF in the space, namely the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT):

It is interesting to notice the very high correlation of 70% during 2025, correlation which has now moved down to 63%. While the correlation has diminished, the high beta nature of the ETF is nonetheless there:

In the past three months the S&P 500 (SPX) via its SPY ETF proxy is up 1.57%. Conversely WGMI is down -20%, while bitcoin is down -35%. WGMI has a very high standard deviation and volatility, with the following metrics extracted from the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab:

The standard deviation is an astounding 85%, while the annualized volatility is 80%.

While as of late the correlation to bitcoin has diminished, the fund beta and volatility are still very high, and generally follow the bitcoin movement. If there is further downside in bitcoin, there is also substantial downside left in WGMI.

Some financials and a potential transformation

Let us look now at some financials for CIFR and IREN to better understand their revenue streams and profitability.

Let us start with the CIFR balance sheet:

As of the latest financials posted to the SEC website, we can see the company running a high outright balance of roughly $170 M in bitcoin, up from the prior year. A significant decrease in bitcoin prices would result in a lower asset value, thus a direct hit to income (unrealized, just like with Strategy).

The CIFR income statement looks as follows:

For the nine months ending September 2025, the company's only source of revenue was bitcoin mining, which brought in $164 M, but not enough to generate a positive net income given the cost structure. In fact Cipher has never posted a positive operating income figure:

However, the company is trying to change this by adjusting its long-term business model. Recently the name has signed a deal with Amazon to provide data center computing power:

Cipher Mining is shifting its focus from pure bitcoin mining to AI enabled data center infrastructure. The company has entered a 15 year lease agreement with Amazon, positioning its facilities for long term data center services.

While this is a positive long-term, we are yet to see that cash-flow hit the revenue line in the income statement and make a difference. Bitcoin is still the dominant force both on the asset side and on the income side.

Now let us have a look at IREN. The balance sheet looks as follows:

IREN has a much larger balance sheet, with no bitcoin on the asset side as per the disclosures above. IREN is actually known for selling its bitcoin as it gains it, making it more of a pure miner rather than an accumulator of the cryptocurrency.

The income statement for IREN is also different:

IREN has a large revenue stream from bitcoin mining, but also an AI cloud services revenue stream. Cloud is only 10% of the revenue stream as of the last quarter, but it is increasing.

When looking historically we can also see that IREN was operationally profitable just one year during its life as a company:

The only time the company was profitable operationally was for the fiscal year ending June 2025, which coincides with the top in bitcoin prices. IREN has also signed a deal with one of the Magnificent 7 in terms of providing cloud infrastructure services:

IREN Limited (NASDAQ: IREN) announced a multi-year agreement with Microsoft valued at approximately $9.7 billion to deliver GPU cloud infrastructure powered by NVIDIA GB300 GPUs. The partnership represents one of the largest AI infrastructure deployments announced this year and reflects the growing demand for high-performance compute.

Under the agreement, IREN will deliver large scale GPU clusters accommodated within IREN’s liquid cooled data centers under construction at its 750 MW campus located in Childress, Texas. The GPUs will be deployed in 4 phases through 2026 (Horizon 1-4) and will collectively provide 200MW of critical IT load. The five-year contract includes a 20 percent prepayment and is expected to contribute roughly $1.94 billion in annualized run rate revenue once fully commissioned.

While it is clear that IREN is diversifying, the operational income picture is not quite there yet in terms of seeing cloud revenues exceed the bitcoin mining ones.

Bitcoin technical take

There are two large bearish technical patterns forming on bitcoin:

Bearish Flag

From a technical standpoint, bitcoin is forming a bearish flag pattern:

A bearish flag is a technical chart pattern signaling a brief pause in a strong downtrend, suggesting the downward trend will likely continue, acting as a continuation pattern where a sharp price drop (flagpole) is followed by a small, upward-sloping consolidation channel (the flag) with decreasing volume, before breaking lower again.

A visual representation of a bearish flag pattern is as follows:

Now let us take a look at what Bitcoin has been doing:

While not every reader might be a fan of technical analysis, patterns usually are correct, especially when they present such a 'clean' take on a theoretical graph.

Head and shoulders

This is another technical trading pattern that can be 'seen' on the bitcoin charts:

The Head & Shoulders pattern is a key chart formation in technical analysis that signals a potential market reversal from an uptrend to a downtrend. It consists of three peaks: a higher middle peak (the "head") flanked by two lower peaks (the "shoulders") and a support line called the "neckline."

From a visual standpoint, the head-and-shoulders pattern looks like this:

The pattern is a bearish one, with most analysts anticipating a decline equal to the change from the neckline to the top. So if the security experienced a +20% gain from the neckline to the top of the head-and-shoulders pattern, then a forecasted -20% loss is penciled in when the neckline is broken. Currently the bitcoin chart looks as follows:

In our view, now that the $75,000 level neckline is broken, the bearish pattern is fully validated. On the downside, some analysts use the difference between the neckline and the top as a downside target.

Conclusion

WGMI is an equities ETF. The name aggregates bitcoin miners in an active portfolio, and its top ten holdings represent over 80% of the fund exposure. IREN and CIFR are currently 20% of the fund each, and the article discusses their income statement and balance sheets. WGMI is highly correlated to bitcoin, and is set to follow the cryptocurrency lower, even as the underlying companies are trying to diversify away. We are a 'Sell' for this name which will continue to follow bitcoin lower.

data.title: Nvidia: The Risk-Reward Is Skewed Into Q4 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
For most of the past two years, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) has been analyzed primarily through the lens of demand—how big the AI opportunity is and how quickly hyperscalers are willing to commit to spending. However, the most relevant shift today is happening on the supply and deployment side of the equation. The current phase of the AI cycle has been much more defined by the physical limits of infrastructure and no longer by speculative enthusiasm.

In a previous work, I approached Nvidia's thesis more from a valuation-sensitive point of view, separating its core business from the optional upside linked to its platform and concluding that there was at the time a meaningful premium in the long-term expectations for mid-2025—which were more modest than they are today.

What has changed today is that earnings and revenue estimates have moved materially higher while the stock has largely traded sideways. This has caused a multiple compression despite improving fundamentals on NVDA shares.

Now, with Q4 and FY2026 results approaching on February 25th, I believe the discussion takes on a tone of whether Nvidia deserves a premium if the market is underestimating how persistent demand remains in a supply-constrained environment. In this context, my stance is shifting from cautious to constructive, and at current levels, I view the risk-reward as skewed to the upside, particularly if execution and guidance confirm that the cycle remains structurally intact.

What’s Actually Different About This AI Cycle

I’ll start by inviting readers to look at Nvidia’s thesis through a less commonly discussed lens. The goal here is simple: to understand what is actually different about this AI cycle.

Although the answer is wide-ranging and can go through many ramifications, I would argue that today it is no longer “strong demand” that has driven the current AI cycle, but rather a change in the economic regime of the technology sector.

In the case of Nvidia, the underlying dynamics have suggested something far more structural is taking place. According to CEO Jensen Huang's own words, the industry is in the middle of a “once-in-a-generation infrastructure buildout”—AI CapEx could exceed $600 billion by 2026, with around 75% linked directly to GPUs, servers, and data centers.

And perhaps herein lies the proof that this is not a normal technology cycle.

One of the strongest signs of this is that even GPUs released six years ago are reportedly increasing in price—and that's quite abnormal in tech.

Normally, technology (1) depreciates, (2) becomes a commodity, and (3) loses value, but not this time. It seems that old hardware is appreciating in value as companies are buying reconditioned A100 and H100 GPUs. Above all, this is ample proof that this is not product cycle behavior but the behavior of a supply-constrained infrastructure asset.

And far be it from me to sound like an AI fanboy. Historically, a supply-constrained infrastructure behavior is something very similar to the era of electrification, the construction of telecom backbones, or even the expansion of AWS infrastructure.

So when legacy hardware appreciates instead of depreciating, we are operating under a scarcity regime.

The physical constraints in this cycle are also visible through the lens of the power requirements of modern AI infrastructure. For example, a single next-generation AI rack could draw close to 100-120 kW, compared to around 5-10 kW in traditional data center environments. This step change in power density shows that the bottleneck comes from the ability to supply electrical power, cooling, and physical capacity at scale and not merely transistor innovation.

It sounds obvious, and perhaps silly, but seeing Nvidia not simply riding a product cycle is the first step to understanding where the company is and how far it can go.

The Physical Limits of Exponential Compute

Recognizing Nvidia as the “official supplier” of constrained compute capacity is only the first step; the next is about whether the physical world is capable of absorbing that capacity at the speed markets currently assume.

Even if demand is absurd, growth can be uneven because deployment is the bottleneck. This isn't about competition, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), China, or even valuation; it's much more about installation, power, cooling, rollout, yield, integration, and so on.

In other words, I don't see any problem in manufacturing the chips, but in making them work at scale under the complexity of integrating thousands of high-density nodes into existing data center environments.

Basically, I see the market today assuming that (1) hyperscalers buy GPUs, (2) revenue appears, and (3) growth is linear. But the actual operational reality involves (1) Blackwell delays, (2) overheating, (3) customers struggling, (4) clusters of 72 GPUs, and so on.

The proof that the bottleneck is real is that power density has indeed exploded, as Blackwell configurations can reach ~132 kW per rack and up to 600 kW-1 MW per rack on the roadmap. This proves that the issue goes beyond server upgrades, as they represent an order-of-magnitude jump in power density that forces a redesign of electrical and cooling systems.

This is where the financial implications come in. Within the CapEx and monetization gap, slow deployment means chips purchased but not yet generating revenue. Indirect evidence of this is that Blackwell had initial rollout difficulties, for example, creating timing mismatches. The side effect ends up being (1) revenue recognition delay for hyperscalers; (2) quarterly volatility with deployment timing interfering with guidance; and (3) margin pressure on hyperscalers with power, cooling, and networking all becoming OpEx.

One of the clearest indicators to show that this is not behaving like a traditional semiconductor cycle is Nvidia's gross margin profile. Even with an unprecedented surge in industry CapEx and a massive ramp in production, Nvidia still manages to sustain gross margins in the 70% range—levels that are much more consistent than a cyclical hardware vendor. In any typical chip cycle, rapid supply expansion would depress pricing and erode margins, not the opposite.

So I'd say that the physical world can't absorb demand at the speed that the market is pricing.

But that doesn't mean that there's any weakening in the AI cycle or that demand is slowing down—in fact, it's still extremely strong. The key point is that the infrastructure needed for this growth has lagged behind the speed at which capital is being invested.

The structural misalignment between orders placed and the actual capacity to put that compute to work is the root cause of the operational volatility the market tends to underestimate when projecting linear growth trajectories.

What Really Matters In Nvidia’s Upcoming Print

That said, going by everything that has already been said, if the cycle is real but the execution is erratic, the market may be looking at the wrong metrics. As Nvidia plans to report its Q4 and full FY2026 earnings on February 25, here are three blocks we can dig into: (1) what the market usually thinks matters for this print; (2) what really matters; and (3) what to expect for 2026.

In the first block, the consensus is going to be around revenue beat or miss, immediate guidance, and Data Center growth headlines, as that's what really drives traditional semiconductor cycles.

But in the second block, I argue that what will really matter in these earnings (based on my thesis) is (1) gross margins, (2) Data Center revenue mix, (3) comments on Blackwell deployment, and (4) lead times/supply constraints.

1. Gross margins:

In the last quarter, Nvidia still managed to post non-GAAP gross margins of 73.6%, only modestly lower YoY, and management pointed to roughly 75% for Q4.

In historical terms, this level of margins is still well above what traditional semiconductor cycles sustain even in peak phases. And the relevant point here goes even further, where this occurs in parallel with an aggressive increase in production and customer CapEx. For Q4, any maintenance of margins close to 75% reinforces the reading that the company continues to operate with exceptional pricing power, while a sharper drop would suggest the start of competitive normalization or commercial concessions to accelerate adoption.

2. Data center revenue:

In fiscal Q3 FY2026, Nvidia reported $51.2 billion in Data Center revenue on a total of $57.0 billion, which implies that the segment accounts for approximately 90% of consolidated revenue.

What really matters for the next print is not just year-over-year growth, but the ability to sustain sequential growth after a quarter that already showed +25% QoQ in the Data Center. If the sequential pace remains high, the market tends to interpret it as a sign of a still robust backlog; if there is a slowdown, the more accurate reading may be one of delivery timing rather than weakening demand, given the disproportionate weight of this segment in total revenue.

3. Blackwell Ramp:

Nvidia has already indicated that the new Blackwell generation has begun to generate billions of dollars in initial sales and that the data center segment associated with the new architecture has shown double-digit sequential growth. The key point for Q4 is to understand how quickly these systems are actually being accepted, installed, and put into production.

For financial modeling, what changes to estimates will tend to be the time between shipment and activation? Any comment indicating longer implementation cycles could lead to a gap between reported revenue and computing capacity that can actually be monetized by customers. On the other hand, evidence of smoother and more consistent ramping would tend to reduce the risk of quarterly volatility associated with large infrastructure projects.

4. Lead times and CapEx of hyperscalers:

On the end demand side, using the estimates that cloud providers and digital platforms are expected to invest between $600 billion and $650 billion in AI infrastructure over the course of 2026, this volume of capital tends to be broadly consistent with the scale of infrastructure currently being deployed.

What would matter here would be signs that lead times remain long or that production remains mostly pre-allocated to reinforce the thesis that the cycle is still underpinned by supply constraints. In contrast, any indication of a significant reduction in delivery times could signal the beginning of a balance between supply and demand, which historically precedes the normalization of margins in hardware cycles.

Finally, entering the third block for Nvidia's fiscal 2027, I believe that, based on the indicators previously mentioned, the greatest probability will tend to be a continuation of high-level growth with greater quarterly variability rather than a cycle slowdown.

I support this hypothesis as the most likely simply because I don't see any other direction when I put the combination of revenue concentrated in the Data Center, margins close to 75%, and record CapEx from clients in the same context. Of course, the conversion of this investment into operational capacity tends to occur in stages, which can produce periods of acceleration followed by temporary normalizations.

Final Remarks: Valuation and Risks

Since June 2025, Nvidia shares have been stuck within the $150-$200 band (essentially fluctuating 35% during this period) despite several positive EPS and revenue revisions over the last six months for FY26 through FY31, and by a high percentage.

In practice, this is a significant contraction of the forward multiple, which makes the valuation even more attractive despite improving fundamentals. Considering $7.71 EPS for FY27, applying a more conservative multiple of 30x would imply a fair price close to $231 per share.

The complementary reading here is the PEG ratio, which remains at 1.0-1.1, significantly below the sector average of 1.5—suggesting that the market already factors in a significant slowdown in earnings growth even with expectations for robust EPS expansion.

To me, this excessive caution makes little sense, especially when we place this valuation within the structural context discussed throughout the article. If the current AI cycle is, in fact, less of a product cycle and more of an infrastructure cycle, then traditional semiconductor multiples are no longer the best benchmark.

What makes the current moment particularly interesting is that, unlike in 2023, investors today are not paying higher multiples for a better story but rather paying lower multiples for stronger fundamentals. I particularly like this, and I believe it makes the window for accumulating Nvidia shares very favorable and supports my bullishness at the moment.

However, I conclude by saying that in the current landscape, the main risk I see to the thesis would be around a faster normalization with a balance between supply and demand for compute. If the production capacity of GPUs, competing solutions, or chips.

The main risk to the thesis is a faster normalization of the balance between supply and demand for compute. If the production capacity of GPUs, competing solutions, or in-house silicon from hyperscalers advances faster than expected, the scarcity that currently sustains Nvidia's pricing power and high margins will logically tend to decrease. In such a scenario, Nvidia would be treated as a cyclical hardware manufacturer and no longer as the “original” and structural infrastructure supplier.

data.title: How I Would Invest $1 Million To Retire On Dividends
data.description:
Warren Buffett once said,

If you don't find a way to make money while you sleep, you will work until you die.

One way to do that is by living beneath your means, saving money, and investing those savings into dividend stocks. Of course, that begs the question, which dividend stocks, and how would you build such a portfolio?

Well, if you have a huge nest egg consisting of millions of dollars, the equation becomes quite simple, as you could easily just buy a low-cost index fund and live off of a very conservative percentage rule with annual withdrawals of 1%, 2%, 3% per year, which would be funded in part by the dividends that the S&P 500 (SPY)(VOO) throws off and the rest from small withdrawals that should be sustainable for the long term. You could even be more conservative and employ an all-weather type portfolio, like Ray Dalio has suggested, to further reduce your sequence of returns risk.

However, for investors with a nest egg that is still substantial, but a little bit more modest, say around $1 million, how would you invest? The math in such a scenario becomes more important in terms of how you generate your passive income. With that in mind, in this article, I'm going to detail how I would invest a $1 million portfolio for retirement.

Initial Assumptions

Before I get into the details, I first want to outline a few assumptions. First of all, many will be retiring on Social Security and potentially have some annuities, a pension, or other sources of income in retirement. Additionally, whether or not you have a mortgage or have to rent your home versus owning it free and clear also matters a lot. Then, of course, there's the question of where you live/cost of living, which can be dictated by both the value of your home, which in turn impacts your property taxes, your home insurance, and home maintenance and utilities costs, as well as which geographic region you live in, as well as your expected quality of life in retirement. All of these have massive impacts on how much you can retire on.

For the purposes of this article, I'm going to look at how I would approach building a portfolio that can generate ~$5,000 a month in passive income from a $1 million portfolio, as when you combine that with other sources of passive and earned income in retirement, as well as potential assumptions around homeownership, owning your home and where you live, that's at least a good starting point.

This is because I believe a 6% yield is very achievable in the current market environment, especially when accounting for wanting it to grow at an annualized rate that meets or beats inflation to make it durable for the long term, while also pursuing adequate diversification.

With those assumptions out of the way, here is how I would break down the portfolio.

Building The Core: Why SCHD Is The Foundation

First of all, my biggest concern would be ensuring I have adequate diversification, and second of all, ensuring that I am generating sufficient dividend growth to keep up with inflation over time. Probably the best single fund to lay the groundwork for that sort of approach is the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), as it combines a dividend yield of over 3.5% on a forward-looking basis with broad diversification across over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks that are also spread across numerous sectors.

Not only that, but SCHD has just a 0.06% expense ratio, making it one of the cheapest ETFs in the entire market, while also having an impressive 10-year dividend growth track record of over 10% CAGR, and growing its dividend every year over its 14-year existence.

The big problem with SCHD, though, is that the dividend yield of between 3.5% and 4% is simply too low to meet the 6% target that we have.

The High-Yield Boost: MLPs And Midstream Powerhouses

Fortunately, the sectors that SCHD lacks much in the way of exposure to are some of the highest-yielding sectors out there, so they can combine with SCHD nicely to bring the yield up to the target 6% level while not having to generate too much dividend growth to still achieve the targeted inflation-meeting or beating dividend growth rate, since SCHD pulls so much of that weight already on the dividend growth front.

My favorite sector to invest in for high yield plus inflation-beating dividend growth right now is the midstream MLP space. If maximizing efficiency is important to you, you can invest in the individual ones like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), MPLX LP (MPLX), Western Midstream Partners (WES), and several others, as these all offer yields in the 6.5% to 9% range and are growing their distributions at a 3% to 12% pace per year, which makes them highly compelling sources of high yield and inflation-beating income. Additionally, their highly contracted cash flows and investment-grade balance sheets make them durable and defensive business models, and they also enjoy growth tailwinds from the energy export boom as well as the AI-fueled natural gas demand boom in the United States right now.

However, it is important to note that these stocks issue K-1 tax forms, so they are not suitable for inclusion in retirement accounts and may not even be suitable for all investors. For those who want to avoid K-1 tax forms, you can just invest in a simple MLP ETF like the Global X MLP ETF (MLPA) or the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), which still can get you around an 8% yield and solid dividend growth, along with diversified exposure to these MLPs. The main drawbacks are that you do pay an expense ratio, and you enjoy fewer tax benefits if you do so. There are also some 1099-issuing midstream companies that are reasonably valued right now and offer fairly attractive dividend yields, like Enbridge Inc. (ENB) and a few others.

However, it is important to note that midstream stocks, while their underlying cash flows are fairly resistant to commodity price swings, their actual stock prices can be highly sensitive to volatile energy prices. Therefore, diversification is quite important. Not only that, but SCHD, while it does not have much in the way of midstream exposure, does have substantial energy sector (XLE) exposure, further emphasizing the importance of diversification beyond midstream companies.

Filling The REIT Gap: Triple-Net And Residential Income

Another sector where SCHD lacks exposure that you can generate an attractive yield from is the REIT sector (VNQ). In particular, for a retiree looking for stable income, I would emphasize some of the blue-chip triple-net lease REITs like Realty Income (O), NNN REIT (NNN), and W. P. Carey (WPC).

Residential REITs are not known for being extremely high-yielding, but you can get some yields between 4% and 5% in the multifamily space right now, like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), or even in the single-family rental space like Invitation Homes (INVH). Additionally, you can further enhance your yield by investing in preferred ETFs focused in the REIT space like the InfraCap REIT Preferred ETF (PFFR), as it offers a near 8% yield right now, or you can even invest in individual REIT preferreds to round out your REIT income yields and bring them up closer to the target rate of 6% or 7%.

Utilities, Infrastructure, And Regulated Stability

Another sector that SCHD lacks much exposure to is infrastructure and regulated utilities (XLU). You can easily meet this simply by investing in a reputable fund like the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG), as it has sustained and grown its dividend for years, dating back to the Great Financial Crisis, and today yields over 6% while having broad diversification across utilities and infrastructure spaces.

Additionally, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC) each yield around 5%, and so they could further round out a portfolio like this. Clearway Energy (CWEN)(CWEN.A) is another attractive dividend-plus-growth option, which offers a yield around our target of 6%.

BDCs And Covered Call ETFs: Yield With Trade-Offs

You can also round out the portfolio with some business development companies (BIZD), as these are also largely missing from SCHD.

The one issue with BDCs, though, is that since they own mostly floating-rate loans in their underlying portfolios, their dividends can be unstable over time. The good news, however, is that these BDCs tend to offer double-digit yields, so you can simply assume that 70% to 80% of their dividend yield is sustainable over time, and the remainder is just extra that can be used to reinvest to drive further dividend growth over time.

The final option that you could pursue to further diversify the portfolio is to buy a small position in a covered call ETF that's focused on mega-cap tech stocks (QQQ), since SCHD lacks that, and it would give you additional diversification in your portfolio.

That being said, I'm not a huge fan of these funds because much of the yield is generated through financial engineering, thereby making it somewhat unreliable over time, and these funds tend to charge relatively high fees and deliver underwhelming total returns relative to their underlying indexes, making them relatively weak risk-reward profile investments.

Still, for an investor who wants a bit more yield and wants the mega-cap tech exposure from the same capital, they could find them useful. While the NEOS NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) has a pretty good track record and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) is also popular, my favorite is the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) due to its having a lower yield than QQQI but implementing a similar strategy, which, in my opinion, is superior over the long term to JEPQ's and certainly better than the Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF's (QYLD).

Beyond Common Stocks: Bonds And Precious Metals

Finally, I would be remiss if I did not mention alternatives outside of preferring common equities as further diversifiers for a portfolio like this.

These can include baby bonds, corporate bonds (LQD), and precious metals (GLD)(SLV) bullion leases, as all of these can further diversify a portfolio, reduce volatility and recession risk, while still generating attractive income yields.

Each investor will have to determine for themselves how much to allocate to these sections, though, because they tend to deliver less dependable dividend growth or no dividend growth at all. They tend to deliver less income growth or, in many cases, no income growth at all, whereas the dividend growth stocks do.

Investor Takeaway

While there are many ways to fund a retirement with passive income, I think building a diversified portfolio of dividend growth stocks with an emphasis on durability and dividend growth that meets or beats inflation over time is one of the best ways to do it on a risk-adjusted basis, as well as without having to commit too much of your time and effort.

Hopefully, the model portfolio offered in this article gives you some good food for thought. Note that it does not reflect all of my top picks, but rather gives a framework and some specific ideas that I like to get you started on your own journey.

This is a similar framework that I apply to building our retirement portfolio that yields between 7% and 8% with above-average total returns and below-market beta at High Yield Investor.

data.title: The London Company Small Cap Vs. Russell 2000 Full Year 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Full Year Market Update

U.S. equities posted strong gains in 2025, marking a third consecutive year of double-digit returns, with the Russell 3000 Index rising 17.2%. The year was shaped by robust earnings growth, continued AI enthusiasm driving technology leadership, and incrementally more supportive monetary policy. However, the market navigated significant volatility, particularly during the spring tariff uncertainty when the S&P 500 fell nearly 19% before recovering. The Federal Reserve reduced rates by 75 basis points in the second half of the year, responding to moderating growth and signs of labor market softening while inflation remained above target. Trade policy uncertainty and tariff developments contributed to periodic volatility, though markets generally proved resilient as implementation timelines shifted.

Like recent years, equity market leadership was again concentrated in Large Cap Growth companies, particularly within technology-oriented areas tied to artificial intelligence, as the equal-weight S&P 500 meaningfully trailed its cap-weighted counterpart. Stylistically, Growth led Value for the year, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Sector performance was mixed, with cyclical leadership led by Communication Services, Technology, and Industrials, while more defensive areas lagged. Among market factors, Growth, Volatility, and Size factors posted the strongest relative returns. Momentum also helped. The Quality and Yield factors were large headwinds. The Value factor was mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

The London Company Small Cap portfolio returned 0.0% (-1.0% net) year-to-date vs. a 12.8% increase in the Russell 2000 Index. Both sector exposure and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance.

Our Small Cap portfolio underperformed for the year and came up short of our upside participation expectations. The full year report card may be disappointing, but it masks the notable developments and context beneath the surface. All the relative weakness occurred between April and October. A historically strong rally in unprofitable, high-beta stocks drove market gains down cap, which our Quality-focused, Lower-Volatility portfolio did not fully capture. Additionally, the portfolio faced sector headwinds, and pockets of idiosyncratic weakness weighed on performance. That said, the portfolio outperformed in Q1 and Q4. It played strong defense amid the volatility early in the year, and it gained ground in the final months as high beta lost traction.

In our view, this is a market where discipline matters more than bold forecasts, and portfolios should be prepared for a wider range of possible outcomes. We believe our Quality-at-a-Reasonable-Price approach may offer participation in market upside while providing the differentiation that may matter most if volatility increases or leadership shifts.

Past performance should not be taken as a guarantee of future results.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) – AWI shares outperformed for the year as they continue to operate well, employing key initiatives to offset a choppy market outlook and low volumes. We continue to like AWI for its consistent execution, strong financials, leading market share and persistent moats through its exclusivity agreements and warranties.

Somnigroup International Inc. (SGI) – SGI continues to perform well despite weak industry demand. Approval of the Mattress Firm acquisition was a major development, providing long-term benefits from vertical integration and materially strengthening the company's competitive position, with early synergies already evident. The combination has the potential to unlock meaningful value, and our thesis is further supported by strong free cash flow generation, brand equity, and solid management execution. We had to exit the name given the market cap over $14B, but continue to hold it in other portfolios.

NewMarket Corporation (NEU) – NEU has been a top performer this year, driven by strong results in its newly acquired defense businesses and stronger profitability in its core business. Continued strength in its rocket business is led by further global conflicts and stockpile replenishment. Finally, the company has been using cash flow to repay outstanding debt, which the market is viewing positively.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

Interparfums, Inc. (IPAR) – IPAR underperformed as a weaker consumer environment slowed the fragrance market, prompting cautious retailer inventory management and near-term destocking. As a result, management has lowered its outlook into 2026. Despite these headwinds, we remain confident in IPAR's ability to deliver above-market growth over time, supported by category expansion, premiumization, and strong brand execution.

Enovis Corporation (ENOV) – ENOV underperformance reflected slower-than-expected growth in orthopedic implants, along with tariff headwinds, acquisition integration costs, and a CEO transition. Despite these near-term pressures, the orthopedic implant portfolio continues to deliver healthy growth and gain market share, while demand for the company's industry-leading bracing products remains resilient. We are confident that ENOV's attractive and diversified orthopedic portfolio supports a long runway for continued earnings growth.

Landstar System, Inc. (LSTR) – LSTR has underperformed this year as the prolonged freight recession, excess capacity, and weak demand continue to pressure volumes and margins across trucking. While near-term conditions remain challenging, the company is focused on cost discipline and operational stabilization, and we believe a normalization in freight demand would highlight the earnings power of LSTR's asset-light, variable cost business model.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Energy (a weaker-performing sector) & overweight Industrials (a better-performing sector)
  • What Hurt: Underweight Health Care (a better-performing sector) & overweight Consumer Staples (a weaker-performing sector)

Portfolio Characteristics & Positioning

We believe the Small Cap portfolio is positioned for long-term durability and possesses the fundamental ingredients that stand the test of time: wide moats, sustainably high returns on capital, strong cash flow generation, low leverage ratios, and trading at a reasonable valuation. As a corporate debt maturity wall approaches and the cost of that capital stays elevated, we believe companies with strong balance sheets and the ability to self-fund their operations could have a structural advantage in 2026 and beyond. In an environment of possibly lower returns and greater volatility, we believe the Small Cap portfolio offers an attractive option for equity investors.

Looking Ahead

As we move into 2026, the economic and policy backdrop remains characterized by a mix of support and uncertainty. On the positive side, corporate earnings trends have remained better than expected, consumer activity has shown resilience, and ongoing Fed rate cuts combined with fiscal stimulus measures may continue to support growth. At the same time, late-cycle dynamics are becoming more evident. Labor market softening, affordability pressures, and persistent housing weakness underscore the uneven nature of growth. Trade and tariff policy remain fluid, and elevated complacency leaves markets vulnerable to adverse surprises. Against this backdrop, economic and inflation data may remain volatile, increasing the likelihood of episodic market dislocations. Despite resilient earnings trends and elevated hyperscaler capital spending, we believe caution is prudent given these crosscurrents.

From an equity market perspective, strong headline returns have masked growing dispersion beneath the surface. Leadership has remained narrow and valuation risk increasingly concentrated, raising the possibility that company-specific risks could take on broader significance. Periodic risk-on rallies, particularly among high-beta and lower-quality segments, have contributed meaningfully to recent performance, but history suggests such episodes are rarely durable. We believe the environment is becoming more conducive to broader leadership and a return to fundamentals, where earnings growth, dividends, and balance-sheet strength matter more than valuation multiple expansion. As volatility rises, downside protection is likely to be driven by companies with high and stable returns on invested capital, conservative leverage, and reasonable valuations. Our portfolios remain anchored in these attributes, which we believe offer a more resilient path through shifting market regimes and help clients participate in long-term wealth creation while managing downside risk.

data.title: AI Is Rocket Fuel For Duolingo
data.description:
Duolingo (DUOL) is currently is a very fortunate position. When most companies talk about AI transformation, they are mainly talking about how AI makes their internal tooling more efficient. In Duolingo's case, AI completely changes the unit-economics of the product itself of making lessons, improving lessons, and scaling lessons into entirely new subjects and domains.

Duolingo does not just benefit from AI internally. Rather, it offers a product which can continually absorb the rapid progress of the latest frontier models with minimal refactors. It also allows the company increase the rate of compounding in which it can improve its own product, through data, iteration speed, and a learning flywheel.

Duolingo's Habit-Building and Size Advantage

Duolingo is now a clear dominant leader in the global gamified learning industry. The company boasts tens of millions of daily users and over 100 million monthly active users. This scale matters because even small improvements in course quality translate to millions of extra practice sessions. These millions of extra practice sessions can then turn into signals in which Duolingo can and does use for improved personalization and teaching.

AI is a Catalyst For Fast Course Creation

To say that AI is beneficial to Duolingo's business model is an understatement. Historically, the largest costs associated with edtech was the human labor associated with created high-quality and tailored course content. AI allows Duolingo to create high-quality content at far faster pace by treating it as an engineering problem: Course experts define course guardrails and goals, and AI helps generate these courses at a fraction at a cost and at far greater speed.

From the consumer's perspective, AI allows Duolingo to address many of the bottlenecks that have plagued the company. Most notably, AI's ability to allow for conversational role-play can vastly improve the language learning experience. Another example is the Explain My Answer feature, which can address issues at the exact moment of confusion. Of course, integrating state-of-the-art AI costs money, however this is offset by the obvious benefits it confers. For instance, Duolingo's Q3 gross margin was still 72.5%, as the management addressed the slight YoY decline to generative AI and hosting costs, while expanding profitability in other areas and improving the product for long-term stickiness.

Birdbrain is Duolingo's Secret Weapon

Despite all the attention on the latest frontier AI models from the likes of OpenAI or Anthropic, Duolingo's in-house personalization model Birdbrain is its real secret weapon. Birdbrain is built to know how difficult a lesson will likely be for an individual and how they should proceed at the right pace.

This is incredibly important as striking the right balance of difficulty for a learner is critical for consumer stickiness. The hard part of this is deciding exactly which exercises are right for the consumers in their current journey. Duolingo's massive scale gives Birdbrain far more chances to figure this formula out that competitors can realistically match. The company is clearly leaning into the scale advantage as it report R&D expenses of $82.7 million in Q3 as it pours resources into the ML infrastructure that makes Birdbrain possible.

The Wrapper Advantage

Unlike many of the doomed players in the AI race, Duolingo does not need to play the frontier model game. All the company needs to do is effectively translate frontier-model capabilities into a delightful customer learning experience and learn/iterate at a faster rate than competitors.

The company has taken advantage of rapidly improving AI capabilities in the premium-tier, and then eventually transferring these advantages to its free tier as models get cheaper over time. While this strategy is far from free, particularly for richer features like conversations, the economics of token consumption are improving at an incredibly fast pace. Duolingo has already proven that it is economical to buy inference at scale, evident in its 29.5% adjusted EBITDA margin in Q3.

Duolingo's Superior Approach

Duolingo's gamified has often been mocked as unserious. However, the more sophisticated interpretation is that Duolingo is actually building a far more effective learning system that optimizes well-researched areas that traditional instruction fails miserably: frequent retrieval practice, immediate feedback, and personalized pacing.

Duolingo's style shines even more with advancing AI capabilities, where the long-lasting criticism of Duolingo's teachings not translating to real-world conversation no longer apply. As was previously mentioned, Duolingo's increasingly adaptive courses, which include conversations and other real-world like scenarios, offer a far greater learning experience. If you believe that AI is trending towards ever-more accurate voice and conversation modes, which is a basically an inevitability at this point, then Duolingo's "game loop" should be able to provide far greater value at scale.

Transforming into a Learning Platform: Beyond Languages

Duolingo has always been seen as just a "language app," which is surprising considering that fact that even its name suggests this to be the case. However, the company is rapidly transitioning to a broader consumer learning ecosystem, again made possible by the rapid advances of AI. The company has successfully expanded its ecosystem to popular subjects like music, math, and chess, while keeping its effective habit loop, game layer, and personalized infrastructure.

If Birdbrain and Duolingo's personalization, every new subject acts as a startup that just compounds on top of Duolingo's growing infrastructure. This kind of optionality is incredibly rare and becomes far more valuable when AI significantly lowers the cost of building high-quality content.

The Flywheel Investment Thesis

The most bullish way to view Duolingo is as a machine that is running a compounding loop where AI increases the slope of improvement and the ability to learn from the learners. In other words, AI makes it far cheaper to generate high-quality content and far richer courses. The data generated from these courses make it easier to personalize, sequence, and retain. These make for a flywheel that make it incredibly difficult for competitors to replicate.

Basically, the flywheel looks something like this. More learners translate to more outcome signals, which translates into better personalization. This improved personalization translates to greater retention, learning outcomes, and even referrals. All of this translates into more revenue in which Duolingo can fund even more product investments, thus strengthening and restarting the loop.

This is made all the more powerful by the fact that education is one of the few areas where even minor product improvements can be measured in learning outcomes and not just some vague engagement metrics.

Risks

The biggest risk facing Duolingo is the near-term reputational damage it can take if it integrates AI poorly. This means that Duolingo has to be incredibly vigilant with the guardrails and prompts they integrate. This risk is especially pronounced as they scale up their AI integrations. Already, we have seen major public backlash regarding Duolingo's use of AI. Many users have reported dissatisfaction with the use of AI in their courses. While this is to be expected in the early stages of such integration, Duolingo still needs to be mindful of AI potential pitfalls if not implemented in a thoughtful manner.

The second major risk to Duolingo is margin pressure from its use of AI. Every AI interaction costs Duolingo in inference costs. In fact, in a released inference customer report from OpenAI, Duolingo was actually the company's top token consumer. If Duolingo does not carefully monitor its token usage, costs could easily spiral out of control. In fact, management already the 2025 margin impact AI as inevitable, but also stated that they were measurable and bounded. FY 2025 experienced a gross margin down by about 80 bps YoY, which much of this directly related to AI spend.

Conclusion

Duolingo's potential comes from the clean fit of what AI is particularly good and what effective gamified products need. Duolingo's years of experience with rapid content iteration, feedback-driven optimization, and personalization make it an increasingly valuable tool with AI acting as a major tailwind. At a market capitalization of $5B and a forward P/E of just 14, Duolingo still has far more room to run.

data.title: Columbia Overseas Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Another quarter, another positive global equity market. For the fourth quarter, developed markets ex-U.S. (per the MSCI EAFE Index) were up 6.13% in local currency terms, although relative strength in the U.S. dollar detracted 1.27% for U.S.-based investors, lowering their return to 4.86%. The full-year 2025 return was 20.60% in local currency and 31.22% in U.S. dollars. Supportive fiscal and monetary policies around the world and the ongoing expectation that policy will continue to be stimulative propelled markets higher during the fourth quarter. Plunging oil prices also contributed to the belief that inflation will stay contained and therefore policy settings can remain favorable. Precious metals prices exploded during the quarter, with gold, silver and platinum up 11.15%, 49.53% and 25.48%, respectively. Interestingly, Bitcoin, often viewed as an alternative to fiat currency similar to precious metals, was actually down 23.55%. Volatility in the equity market was well contained, although bond market volatility, represented by the Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate (MOVE) index, continued to fall toward historic lows.

From a country perspective, all developed markets were positive during the quarter, with the exception of Australia, when measured in local currency terms. Austria, Finland and Ireland led the way, up 17.97%, 14.32% and 14.29%, respectively. Australia was the worst-performing developed market, down 1.57%, with New Zealand up 0.58% and Portugal up 0.74%. On a sector basis, utilities and health care were the standout performers, up 10.63% and 10.39% in local currencies, followed by financials, up 8.37%. Communication services was the biggest laggard and the only sector with a negative return, down 5.01%, followed by real estate, up 2.59%, and consumer discretionary, up 3.62%.

Quarterly portfolio recap

From a sector perspective, security selection drove the fund's relative underperformance, particularly within the consumer discretionary, consumer staples and information technology sectors. Security selection contributed within industrials, real estate and materials. An overweight to communication services contributed, while an overweight to energy detracted.

In country terms, positioning in the United Kingdom, Israel and Greece detracted. Positioning in Ireland, Canada and Australia contributed.

Quarterly contributors to absolute and relative results

  • Canadian silver miner Pan American Silver jumped in value as the stock took part in a standout quarter for silver and other precious metals.

  • Global biopharma company Jazz Pharmaceuticals spiked on a positive trial result for a key pipeline drug.

Quarterly detractors to absolute and relative results

  • Greek bank Piraeus modestly pulled back after the stock nearly doubled over the first nine months of the year.
  • Despite posting decent results in November, Japanese retail-drug-store operator MatsukiyoCocokara continued a decline that began after a letdown earnings report in August. Investors also anticipated that strained China-Japan relations could affect short-term performance.
  • British litigation finance provider Burford Capital posted a disappointing earnings report amid uncertainty in the company's ongoing legal battle with the country of Argentina.

Outlook

Looking forward, we expect the economic backdrop characterized by slow, steady growth to continue, implying the same steady trajectory for aggregate earnings growth. Therefore, trends in markets are likely to be driven by monetary and fiscal policy decisions, which provide the liquidity backdrop that often determines the multiple ascribed to those earnings. Policy settings were largely supportive during the second half of 2025; given the stabilization in inflation trends, even where above target, we see those favorable policy settings continuing as we enter 2026. We believe it will be the politics of inflation (more precisely, the post-COVID jump in the cost of living) that will ultimately constrain the ability of policymakers to continue fiscal and monetary policy support and therefore mark the end of this incredible post-COVID bull market.
